title: Calculating Discounts at the Mall - A Life Lesson
excerpt: A 30% off sign is a great percentage exercise. The 10% trick that solves almost everything, a worked after-discount price example, and the mistake almost everyone makes.
author: Smart Worksheets Team
content:
Every Sale Sign Is a Percentage Exercise

Percentages sound abstract and scary to kids, until they reach the mall. There, suddenly, 30% off is not a problem in a book, it is how much money stays in their pocket. Shopping is the best way to teach percentages, because the result is real and the child genuinely cares about it.

The Ten Percent Trick

This is the whole secret, and it is worth teaching first. To find 10% of a number, just move the decimal point one place to the left, or in plain words, drop a zero. 10% of 200 dollars is 20 dollars. 10% of 80 is 8. Once the child masters 10%, they can build almost any percentage. 30% is three times 10%. 20% is twice. 5% is half of 10%.

Let Us Work Out an After-Discount Price

A shirt at 80 dollars with 30% off. First find 10%, which is 8 dollars. Now times three, 8 times 3 equals 24 dollars off. Finally, the price after the discount, 80 minus 24 equals 56 dollars. Let your child do this at the register before the cashier rings it up, and it turns into a suspenseful game.

A Who-Calculates-Faster Race

At the mall, in front of a sale sign, race to reach the final price first. You can also ask which deal is better, 25% off or buy one get the second at half price. Questions like these activate real math and consumer thinking at the same time.

A Percent Is Always Out of Something

One point is worth sharpening, because it is a source of confusion. A percent on its own says nothing, it is always a percent of a certain number. 30% of 80 dollars is 24, but 30% of 200 dollars is already 60. The same percent, completely different amounts. Ask your child questions that sharpen this, which is better, 50% off a 40-dollar shirt or 20% off the same shirt at 100 dollars. They will discover that the bigger percent does not always mean the bigger saving.

The Mistake Almost Everyone Makes

The classic mix-up, calculate the discount and forget to subtract it from the price. The child finds that 30% of 80 is 24 and writes 24 as the answer, but 24 is the size of the discount, not the price to pay. There is always a last step, subtract the discount from the original price. It is worth repeating this every time.

Questions Parents Ask

At what age are percentages taught? Percentages usually come in fifth and sixth grade, after fractions and decimals are settled, because a percent is really a fraction out of a hundred.

My child understands 10% but gets stuck on odd ones like 15%, what do I do? Break it down. 15% is 10% plus 5%, and 5% is half of 10%. Every percent is built from familiar pieces.

How do we practice at home? You can practice on real deals in a store flyer, and also create percentage exercises in the percentage generator to lock in the method.
